The worsening parameter remains #39 (productivity/airflow) because any mitigation of chemical emissions typically reduces airflow or adds complexity. 2) A fresh matrix lookup for (31,39) still suggests principles including #28, #19, but also #35 (Parameter changes) and #3 (Local quality) as additional recommendations. Under the constraint, #28 (Mechanics substitution) must be extended: we still replace the fan, but now we must substitute the EHD electrodes and materials with VOC-inert, non-outgassing alternatives. #19 (Periodic action) is retained but modified: pulsing must be optimized not just for ozone control but also to minimize peak voltages that drive outgassing. #35 (Parameter changes) is newly applied: change the physical state of the airflow medium (e.g., use a temperature or humidity gradient to drive flow) or change the electrode material to a catalytic surface that decomposes ozone instantly. #3 (Local quality) is applied to create a non-uniform electric field that ionizes air only in a sealed, catalytically-coated microchannel, isolating any potential VOC sources from the clean-room environment. 3) The constraint forces the selection of #35 and #3 because the original principles alone cannot address the chemical emission issue without compromising airflow. #35 suggests altering the fundamental parameters of the system (e.g., switching from corona discharge to a dielectric barrier discharge with a VOC-absorbing dielectric). #3 introduces structural non-uniformity to confine and neutralize emissions locally. 4) Thus, the evolved solution integrates all four principles to achieve silent, safe, VOC-free air movement.
cases.solution: A clean-room-compatible desktop air purifier using a dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) ionic wind pump. The airflow channel is lined with a nanoporous zeolite-impregnated ceramic that catalytically decomposes ozone and adsorbs any trace VOCs. The DBD electrodes are made of gold-plated titanium to prevent corrosion and outgassing, and are embedded in the channel walls (Local quality). The system operates in a pulsed bipolar mode (Periodic action) with carefully controlled voltage, frequency, and duty cycle to maximize airflow (Productivity) while minimizing chemical byproducts. The entire assembly is housed in electropolished stainless steel and sealed with metal gaskets for zero VOC emissions, meeting ISO 14644-1 Class 5 requirements.

cases.problem: Bug report #PRJ-882: CPU cooling fan for the 'Nimbus' mini-PC. Current model (Axial-7B, 92mm, 2500 RPM) has a measured SPL of 42 dBA at 1m, exceeding the 35 dBA design spec. Root cause: blade-pass frequency at 2500 RPM generates a 583 Hz tonal peak. Additionally, 3 user injury reports filed in Q3 due to fingers contacting blades through the top vent during maintenance. Increasing RPM to 3000 for the new 65W chip would raise SPL to an estimated 46 dBA and worsen the injury risk. The proposed synthetic jet actuator array eliminates rotating blades and reduces noise to 28 dBA, but introduces a new hidden constraint: the high-velocity vortex rings entrain dust and debris from the environment, rapidly clogging the 2mm orifices over time. Field data from similar piezoelectric actuators shows a 40% reduction in airflow after 500 hours of operation in typical home/office dust conditions, leading to inadequate cooling and potential CPU throttling. This dust accumulation problem must be solved without reintroducing moving parts, significantly increasing power consumption, or compromising the acoustic performance.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: The rotating blades are the source of both noise (42 dBA, 583 Hz peak) and laceration risk (harmful factors #31). The need to cool a more powerful 65W chip demands higher airflow (productivity #39), which directly increases RPM, noise, and danger. The TRIZ matrix for improving #31 without sacrificing #39 points to principles #28 and #19. Solution: Replace the rotary fan with a synthetic jet actuator (Mechanics substitution #28) using a piezoelectric diaphragm oscillating at 180 Hz (Periodic action #19) to generate high-momentum vortex rings. This yields 28 dBA SPL and zero blade risk. 2) Secondary contradiction: The synthetic jet solution creates a new problem—the small orifices are prone to dust clogging, reducing reliability (#27) over time. Addressing this typically requires adding filters, self-cleaning mechanisms, or larger orifices, which increases device complexity (#36) and may raise cost, size, or power draw. The TRIZ matrix for improving reliability (#27) without increasing device complexity (#36) suggests principle #22 (Blessing in disguise). 3) Combined resolution: Apply #22 to the dust accumulation problem. Instead of fighting dust, we harness it. The synthetic jet actuator is redesigned with a slightly larger orifice (3mm) and a textured inner surface. The periodic 180 Hz pulsation (#19) causes the orifice to act as a self-cleaning, acoustic dust trap. Dust particles entrained in the incoming air are accelerated and impact the textured walls, where they adhere due to van der Waals forces, gradually building a porous, sound-absorbing lining. This lining actually improves the Helmholtz resonance matching over time, reducing noise further. The mechanism requires no additional moving parts, preserving the mechanics substitution (#28). A secondary, lower-amplitude 20 Hz cleaning pulse is introduced every 10 minutes to dislodge any loose aggregates, which are then ejected as harmless micro-clumps. Thus, the system maintains >95% airflow capacity after 5000 hours, meets the 35 dBA spec with an even lower 25 dBA after dust lining formation, and adds negligible complexity.
cases.solution: A self-dust-utilizing synthetic jet CPU cooler. A 4-cell array uses 180 Hz piezoelectric diaphragms to generate vortex rings through 3mm orifices with micro-textured internal walls. Ingested dust adheres to the walls, forming a porous acoustic liner that enhances resonance and reduces noise to 25 dBA over time. A periodic 20 Hz, low-amplitude cleaning pulse ejects excess dust without affecting cooling. The system has no rotating parts, maintains >95% airflow after 5000 hours in dusty environments, and requires no external filters.

cases.problem: Specification: A 400V Li-ion traction battery module (12s3p, 4.2 kWh) must maintain cell-to-cell temperature delta < 2°C under a 2C continuous discharge at 45°C ambient, while surviving 10,000 g shock per MIL-STD-810. Current cold-plate-based liquid cooling achieves 3.5°C delta due to flow maldistribution, consuming 150 W pump power, and uses a thick thermal interface material (TIM) with 0.8 K/W contact resistance. The original solution of a segmented, manifold-microchannel composite cold plate with compliant graphite-polymer frame eliminates TIM and reduces pump power, but the thin-walled microchannels and compliant composite frame are structurally vulnerable to extreme shock loads, risking coolant leakage or loss of thermal contact. Requirement: reduce temperature non-uniformity to < 2°C without increasing pump power or reducing module volumetric energy density (210 Wh/L), while ensuring structural integrity under 10,000 g shock.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Concrete: The parent solution introduces thin-walled microchannels and a compliant composite frame to achieve thermal uniformity, but these features are inherently fragile under 10,000 g shock, risking structural failure and coolant loss. 2) The constraint forces a re-evaluation of the contradiction. Improving parameter remains Shape (#12) because we still need a geometry that ensures uniform cell contact and flow distribution. Worsening parameter remains Loss of energy (#22) because added structural reinforcement typically increases mass or pump work. However, the constraint introduces a hidden secondary contradiction: improving Strength (#14) worsens Shape (#12) or Loss of energy (#22), but the primary matrix lookup for (12,22) is still valid as the dominant pair. 3) TRIZ matrix lookup for (12,22) yields {1, 17, 35, 28, 40}. The parent already used {1, 17, 35, 40}. The shock constraint demands principles that address structural integrity without compromising the thermal-fluidic gains. 4) Principle #14 (Spheroidality - Curvature) is introduced to replace flat, thin-walled channels with arched or domed microchannel cross-sections, which inherently resist shock loads by distributing stress. 5) Principle #28 (Mechanics substitution) is added to replace the purely mechanical compliant frame with a field-assisted (e.g., magnetic or electrostatic) clamping mechanism that maintains contact pressure during shock without adding brittle mass. 6) Principles #1, #17, #35, and #40 are retained but reinterpreted under the constraint: Segmentation (#1) now includes shock-isolated sub-modules; Transition to a new dimension (#17) uses 3D-printed lattice structures that are both lightweight and shock-absorbent; Parameter changes (#35) includes a shear-thickening nanofluid that solidifies under high strain rates to dampen shock; Composite materials (#40) now incorporates ceramic-fiber reinforcement in the graphite-polymer to increase toughness. 7) Result: a shock-tolerant thermal management system that maintains <2°C delta-T at 45 W pump power and survives 10,000 g shock through arched microchannels, magnetically clamped conformal interfaces, and a shear-thickening coolant.
cases.solution: A shock-hardened segmented manifold-microchannel composite cold plate: each cell face contacts a dedicated microchannel tile with arched channel cross-sections (spheroidal curvature) to resist shock. Tiles are embedded in a ceramic-fiber-reinforced graphite-polymer composite frame that uses embedded permanent magnets to maintain constant contact pressure against cell surfaces, eliminating TIM and accommodating shock-induced displacements. A shear-thickening dielectric nanofluid (e.g., silica nanoparticles in low-viscosity base fluid) is used as coolant, which remains low-viscosity during normal operation but solidifies under high-frequency shock to dampen vibrations and prevent leakage. Hierarchical 2.5D manifold distributes flow evenly. Achieves <2°C cell delta-T at 45 W pump power, 210 Wh/L, and survives 10,000 g shock per MIL-STD-810.

cases.problem: This study addresses the challenge of thermal non-uniformity in high-energy-density lithium-ion battery packs for electric vehicles, where conventional cooling plate geometries induce significant temperature gradients and parasitic energy consumption. The core conflict exists between the geometric configuration of the heat removal structure and the system-level energy efficiency. We propose a novel thermal management architecture that decouples local heat acquisition from global heat rejection through a segmented, multi-dimensional composite structure. However, the system must also survive a 10,000 g mechanical shock per MIL-STD-810, which introduces a critical conflict: the complex, thin-walled 3D manifold and functionally graded composite, optimized for thermal performance, are inherently vulnerable to fracture or delamination under high-g loading, threatening structural integrity and loss of thermal function.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original problem focused on the contradiction (Shape vs. Loss of energy) using principles {1, 17, 35, 40} to achieve thermal uniformity and efficiency. 2) The applied constraint (MIL-STD-810 10,000 g shock) introduces a new, dominant contradiction: the complex 3D manifold geometry (Shape #12) directly compromises mechanical Strength (#14) under high-g loads. This shifts the worsening parameter from 'Loss of energy' to 'Strength', as structural failure becomes the primary risk. 3) A fresh TRIZ matrix lookup for the pair (12,14) yields inventive principles {1, 15, 34, 40, 9, 17}. 4) Principle #1 (Segmentation) is retained but now applied mechanically: dividing the manifold into smaller, shock-isolated modules reduces stress concentrations and allows independent motion. 5) Principle #17 (Transition to a new dimension) is reinterpreted to include 3D interlocking geometries for mechanical interlocking, not just thermal flow. 6) Principle #40 (Composite materials) is maintained but the functional grading must now incorporate energy-absorbing phases (e.g., ceramic or elastomeric inclusions) for shock dissipation. 7) Principle #9 (Preliminary anti-action) is newly introduced by pre-stressing the composite or incorporating sacrificial buckling zones that absorb shock energy before it reaches critical thermal pathways. 8) Principle #15 (Dynamics) is added to make the manifold mounting flexible or allow controlled movement during shock, decoupling the thermal structure from the shock load path. 9) Principle #35 (Parameter changes) is deprioritized because two-phase flow-boiling, while thermally efficient, introduces pressure pulsations and vapor void fractions that exacerbate shock-induced fatigue; a single-phase liquid cooling with high-thermal-capacity nanofluids is selected for robustness. 10) The resulting solution integrates mechanical and thermal functions, achieving both thermal uniformity (σ_T < 1.5°C) and shock survival.
cases.solution: A shock-hardened BTMS using segmented, interlocking 3D manifold modules fabricated from a functionally graded AlSi10Mg composite with embedded energy-absorbing ceramic micro-lattices and elastomeric interlayers. Each module is mounted on dynamic vibration isolators that allow limited motion during 10,000 g shocks. Cooling employs a single-phase nanofluid (e.g., Al2O3-water) in interpenetrating channels to avoid two-phase instability. The design achieves cell temperature uniformity (σ_T = 1.4°C) and survives MIL-STD-810 shock testing without coolant leakage or structural failure.

cases.problem: A high-voltage transmission tower requires a corona ring to suppress partial discharge. The metallic ring, mounted on insulators, creates a parasitic capacitance that distorts the electric field measurement data from the adjacent optical current transformer (OCT), reducing grid monitoring accuracy. Additionally, the entire assembly must be sterilisable by gamma irradiation without degradation, as mandated for equipment deployed in biologically sensitive or quarantine zones. The original field-grading semi-conductive glaze solution degrades under gamma radiation, losing its humidity-dependent switching properties and compromising both corona suppression and measurement fidelity.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Original contradiction: corona ring suppresses harmful discharge (#30) but its metal mass distorts sensor signals causing information loss (#24). The parent solution used semi-conductive glaze with humidity-sensitive switching (principles #6 Universality, #13 Inversion, #19 Periodic action). 2) The gamma sterilization constraint (ISO 11137) introduces a new material degradation conflict: the semi-conductive polymer matrix in the glaze cross-links or chain-scissions under radiation, destroying its reversible humidity response. This eliminates #19 Periodic action (time-dependent activation) and #6 Universality (multi-functionality) because the glaze cannot maintain dual functionality post-sterilization. 3) Re-evaluating the contradiction matrix with the same parameters but now under material stability requirements, principles #35 Parameter change, #28 Mechanics substitution, #2 Taking out, and #13 Inversion emerge as more relevant. 4) #35 suggests changing the physical state or material property to withstand radiation—use an inorganic, radiation-hard material. #28 implies replacing a mechanical (solid-state) field control with a field-based or optical method. #2 recommends separating the problematic part (the corona suppression function) from the sensitive measurement zone. #13 inverts the problem: instead of adding a ring or coating, remove material to shape the field. 5) By applying these, we can eliminate organic materials and separate the field-grading function physically from the OCT, ensuring gamma stability and measurement accuracy.
cases.solution: Replace the corona ring and any organic coatings with a radiation-hard, inorganic field-grading system: install a series of floating, capacitively-coupled metal grading rings made of gamma-sterilizable stainless steel, but physically relocate them to the tower cross-arm, far from the OCT's optical path. The rings are shaped via parametric optimization (applying #13 Inversion to remove material where field distortion is critical) to grade the insulator string potential without encroaching on the OCT's sensing volume. This 'taking out' (#2) of the field-grading structure eliminates parasitic capacitance at the measurement point, while the all-metal, ceramic-insulated assembly tolerates gamma irradiation without degradation.

cases.problem: A railway catenary system uses a supplementary earth wire for lightning protection, strung above the contact wire. Post-installation, the track circuit signaling system shows a 12.7% increase in false 'track occupied' readings during thunderstorms. Analysis confirms the earth wire is electromagnetically coupling the lightning transient's noise into the 50 Hz track circuit, causing a signal-to-noise ratio drop from 28 dB to 9 dB. The initial solution replaces the dedicated earth wire with a messenger wire on surge-arrester insulators that only ground during overvoltage. However, during a lightning strike, the spark gap conducts and the messenger wire becomes a temporary earth wire, but the transient grounding current now flows through the messenger wire, inducing a high-voltage pulse into the contact wire via mutual inductance, which can damage train-borne electronics and momentarily disrupt the traction power supply. This reveals a hidden constraint: the messenger wire's dual function creates a new coupling path during the very event it is meant to protect against.
cases.reasoning_chain: Primary contradiction: Earth wire mitigates lightning damage (#30, Object-affected harmful factors) but EM coupling reduces track circuit signal integrity (#24, Loss of information). Matrix lookup for #30 vs #24 suggests #6 Universality, #13 Inversion, #19 Periodic action. Initial solution applies these: messenger wire acts as temporary earth wire via spark gap (Inversion, Periodic action), eliminating continuous noise path (Universality). Secondary contradiction emerges: The messenger wire's dual function reduces device complexity (#36) by eliminating a separate wire, but during lightning strike the transient current in the messenger wire induces harmful voltage in the contact wire (#31, Harmful side effects). Matrix lookup for #36 vs #31 yields #2 Extraction, #24 Intermediary, #40 Composite materials. To resolve both contradictions, we apply #2 Extraction: separate the lightning current path from the messenger wire by using a dedicated, insulated down conductor inside the messenger wire's cross-section. #24 Intermediary: insert a saturable magnetic core around the messenger wire that acts as a transient current limiter, preventing high di/dt coupling during strikes while remaining transparent to normal traction current. #40 Composite materials: construct the messenger wire as a composite of conductive strands and high-permeability magnetic material, forming a distributed inductance that chokes lightning transients. This combined approach maintains the primary solution's benefit (no continuous noise path) while mitigating the secondary harmful coupling, preserving both signal integrity and equipment protection.
cases.solution: Replace the solid messenger wire with a composite coaxial conductor: an inner steel core for strength, a layer of high-permeability magnetic tape, and an outer aluminum layer for conductivity. The magnetic layer acts as a distributed surge impedance, limiting the rate of current rise during a lightning strike. Additionally, a saturable-core reactor is installed at each support mast in series with the spark gap. Under normal conditions, the reactor presents high impedance to 50 Hz, forcing the messenger wire to remain isolated. During a lightning overvoltage, the spark gap conducts and the reactor saturates, providing a low-impedance path to ground while its inductance smooths the current waveform, drastically reducing the induced voltage in the contact wire. This eliminates the need for a separate earth wire, prevents continuous EMI, and protects against transient coupling.

cases.problem: A railway catenary system uses a supplementary earth wire for lightning protection, strung above the contact wire. Post-installation, the track circuit signaling system shows a 12.7% increase in false 'track occupied' readings during thunderstorms. Analysis confirms the earth wire is electromagnetically coupling the lightning transient's noise into the 50 Hz track circuit, causing a signal-to-noise ratio drop from 28 dB to 9 dB. The originally proposed solution of spark-gap equipped messenger wire insulators is now constrained by a requirement for zero volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions during production and installation, mandating a clean-room manufacturing environment that prohibits solvent-based coatings, adhesives, or outgassing materials typically used in high-voltage insulator fabrication.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original solution relied on surge-arrester insulators with spark gaps, but their manufacturing typically uses epoxy resins, silicone coatings, or solvent-based adhesives that emit VOCs, violating the clean-room constraint. This shifts the contradiction from a purely electromagnetic interference (EMI) problem to a materials and manufacturing compatibility problem. 2) The improving parameter (Object-affected harmful factors, #30) remains the same: we must still protect against lightning while preventing false track occupancy. The worsening parameter (Loss of information, #24) also remains, as any solution must not degrade signal integrity. However, the constraint introduces a hidden worsening feature: 'Ease of manufacture' (#32) or 'Adaptability' (#35) could be considered, but the primary worsening is still Loss of information because the noise coupling path must be eliminated without VOC-emitting materials. 3) A fresh TRIZ matrix lookup for #30 vs. #24 yields principles #1, #24, #28, #33, #40. Given the VOC constraint, #40 Composite materials becomes highly relevant: we can design insulators from VOC-free, non-outgassing composites that still provide high-voltage insulation and controlled breakdown. 4) Principle #2 Taking out is applied to separate the problematic component: instead of integrating the spark gap into a coated insulator, we remove the gap from the solid dielectric and create an air gap with electrodes made from inert, clean-manufactured materials (e.g., stainless steel, ceramic). 5) Principle #19 Periodic action is retained from the parent because the spark gap's intermittent operation remains essential—it only conducts during lightning surges, avoiding continuous EMI. 6) The combination of #40, #2, and #19 allows us to meet the zero-VOC requirement while preserving the functional spark-gap concept.
cases.solution: Replace the surge-arrester insulators with a two-part assembly: a machined ceramic insulating body (fired in a clean kiln, zero VOC) and a pair of stainless steel electrodes forming an air spark gap. The ceramic is unglazed and requires no coatings; the electrodes are mechanically clamped, eliminating adhesives. The assembly is mounted on each mast, connecting the messenger wire to ground via the air gap. Under normal voltage, the gap isolates the wire; during a lightning strike exceeding 15 kV, the gap arcs over, providing a temporary earth path. The entire production and installation process is solvent-free and compatible with a clean-room environment.

cases.problem: This study addresses the electromagnetic incompatibility between lightning protection systems (LPS) and co-sited structural health monitoring (SHM) networks on long-span bridges. A conventional Franklin rod array, while effectively mitigating the harmful effects of direct lightning attachment (improving parameter #30), introduces significant passive intermodulation (PIM) distortion and scattering, which corrupts the low-power SHM wireless sensor data streams, leading to an unacceptable loss of information (worsening parameter #24). The original solution of a retractable, reel-based conductor system deployed by an early-warning network fails under the mandated automotive-grade temperature range of -40°C to +125°C, as the mechanical deployment mechanism (motors, reels, telescopic segments) suffers from lubricant freezing, differential thermal expansion, and material embrittlement, causing unreliable operation and potential jamming precisely when lightning protection is needed.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The constraint of -40°C to +125°C renders the original mechanical retractable solution (based on principles #6, #13, #19) impractical due to reliability issues with moving parts in extreme temperatures. 2) The original contradiction pair (#30 vs #24) remains valid, but the solution space must avoid temperature-sensitive mechanisms. 3) The TRIZ matrix for this pair suggests principles #6, #13, #19, but these must be reinterpreted or replaced under the constraint. 4) Principle #35 (Parameter change) is introduced to alter the physical state or properties of the system to eliminate mechanical movement—e.g., using a material that changes conductivity or shape with temperature or electric field. 5) Principle #10 (Preliminary action) is applied to pre-arrange the necessary lightning protection elements in a dormant, non-interfering state that activates without mechanical motion. 6) Principle #24 (Intermediary) uses an intermediate medium or field to transfer the lightning current without physical contact or permanent structures. 7) Principle #13 (Inversion) is retained but shifted from mechanical inversion (absent/present rods) to functional inversion: the bridge structure itself becomes the protection only under lightning conditions via a reversible, solid-state process. 8) These principles collectively eliminate the need for motors, reels, and telescoping parts, ensuring reliable operation across the full temperature range while still resolving the electromagnetic incompatibility.
cases.solution: A solid-state adaptive lightning protection system integrated into the bridge's structural composite. The bridge's non-conductive fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) components are embedded with a network of field-responsive conductive polymer strips. Under normal conditions, these strips are in a high-resistance, RF-transparent state, causing no interference to SHM signals. An electric field mill network detects imminent lightning and triggers a low-voltage bias that switches the polymer strips to a highly conductive state via a reversible electrochemical doping mechanism, forming a preferential, distributed lightning channel along the bridge profile. After the strike, the bias is removed, and the strips return to their non-interfering state, all without any moving parts, functioning reliably from -40°C to +125°C.

cases.problem: A beverage canning line for carbonated fruit juice operates at 2,200 cans per minute. The liquid filler nozzle must retract before the seamer engages, but the dwell time of the mechanical cam-driven retraction system limits the maximum cycle rate to 1,800 cpm. The primary solution replaces the cam with an integrated magnetic linear actuator, achieving 8 ms retraction. However, this high-speed magnetic actuation generates intense localized heat in the filler valve body (exceeding 150°C), which warms the juice by 3–5°C during filling. This temperature rise accelerates CO2 breakout, causing foaming, inconsistent fill levels, and a new 8% reject rate due to underfilled or overfoamed cans. The hidden constraint is that the faster, contactless retraction conflicts with thermal stability of the product.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: improving Productivity (#39) vs worsening Manufacturability (#32). Matrix lookup for (39,32) yields principles #35 Parameter changes, #10 Preliminary action, #5 Merging. Applied by replacing mechanical cam with magnetic actuator (#35), pre-charging coil (#10), and integrating actuator into valve body (#5). This resolves vibration and speed limitation. 2) Secondary contradiction emerges: the magnetic actuator's rapid operation (improving Speed #9) generates excessive heat, worsening Temperature (#17) of the product, causing foaming and rejects. Matrix lookup for (9,17) yields principles #2 Taking out, #25 Self-service, #35 Parameter changes. 3) Combine principles: #2 Taking out suggests removing the heat source from the product zone—relocate the magnetic coil outside the product-contact area or decouple it thermally. #25 Self-service suggests using the waste heat or product itself to manage the issue—e.g., a phase-change material (PCM) jacket that absorbs heat without external energy. #35 Parameter changes is reused to alter the thermal state: switch from resistive-loss-prone copper coil to a superconducting or high-efficiency pulse coil, reducing heat generation. 4) Integrated solution: redesign the actuator as a two-part module—a remote, thermally isolated drive coil that magnetically couples to a lightweight nozzle carrier inside the filler. The carrier incorporates a sealed PCM chamber (e.g., paraffin wax) that melts during actuation, absorbing peak heat. Pulse the coil with ultra-short, high-current discharges (preliminary action #10) to minimize RMS heating. This resolves both contradictions: productivity and manufacturability via magnetic actuation, and speed vs temperature via thermal decoupling and self-cooling.
cases.solution: Implement a split magnetic actuator: a stationary drive coil located outside the filler body, magnetically coupled to a moving nozzle carrier. The carrier is thermally isolated and contains a phase-change material (PCM) jacket that absorbs actuation heat. The coil is pulsed with pre-charged, ultrashort current bursts, reducing average heat generation. This enables 2,200 cpm operation with <0.1% reject rate and maintains juice temperature within ±0.5°C, eliminating foaming.

cases.problem: A beverage canning line for carbonated fruit juice operates at 2,200 cans per minute. The liquid filler nozzle must retract before the seamer engages, but the dwell time of the mechanical cam-driven retraction system limits the maximum cycle rate to 1,800 cpm. Increasing cam speed induces vibration and misalignment, causing 12% reject rate due to crushed can flanges. The original solution replaces the cam with a magnetic linear actuator using rare-earth magnets and encapsulated coils. However, the EU Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) 2024 mandates that all components must be fully recyclable at end-of-life. The magnetic actuator contains bonded NdFeB magnets and epoxy-potted copper windings that are inseparable, preventing material recovery and recyclability. This creates a conflict between achieving high-speed actuation and meeting mandatory recyclability requirements.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction was Productivity (#39) vs. Manufacturability (#32), solved with magnetic actuation (principles #35, #10, #5). 2) The recyclability constraint introduces a new worsening parameter: the bonded magnetic actuator is not recyclable, which impairs the system's Adaptability (#35) to end-of-life requirements. The original worsening parameter (Manufacturability) is less critical now because the magnetic solution solved the vibration issue, but the recyclability failure makes the system non-compliant. 3) Thus, the contradiction shifts to Productivity (#39) vs. Adaptability (#35). Matrix lookup for (39,35) yields principles #28, #15, #10. 4) Principle #28 (Mechanics substitution) suggests replacing the mechanical cam with another field — but the constraint forbids non-recyclable electromagnetic components. Instead, we apply #28 to use a recyclable mechanical field: a compressed air pulse (pneumatic) that is inherently separable and recyclable (metal valve, polymer seals easily disassembled). 5) Principle #15 (Dynamics) suggests making the retraction system adjustable or self-optimizing — we design a pneumatic actuator with adaptive damping that tunes itself to the canning speed, eliminating vibration without bonded parts. 6) Principle #10 (Preliminary action) is retained: pre-compress air in a reservoir before each can arrives, enabling instantaneous retraction. 7) The original principles #35 and #5 are deprioritized because #35 (Parameter change) to magnetic fields conflicts with recyclability, and #5 (Merging) would integrate components into a non-separable module. 8) The new solution uses a disassembleable, all-metal pneumatic actuator with standard O-ring seals, achieving 8 ms retraction and full material recovery.
cases.solution: Replace the mechanical cam with a high-speed pneumatic retraction system using a pre-charged air reservoir and a lightweight aluminum piston. The actuator body, piston, and seals are designed for rapid disassembly (snap-fit or threaded connections) enabling complete material separation for recycling. The system achieves 8 ms retraction, supports 2,200 cpm, and meets EU ESPR 2024 recyclability requirements.

cases.problem: We're shrink-wrapping frozen pizzas hot off the oven belt to save reheating energy. The original fix uses a water misting bar to create a steam cushion that prevents cheese from sticking to the plastic film, but that solution relies on a multi-material assembly — the misting bar, integrated guide, and the film itself — which complicates end-of-life separation. Under the EU Ecodesign Regulation 2024, packaging must be fully recyclable without requiring disassembly into different material streams. The current shrink film with food residue and the integrated misting unit create a mixed waste problem. We need a way to maintain full-speed wrapping without sticking while ensuring the entire packaging system is mono-material or easily separable for recycling.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction was Productivity (#39) vs. Manufacturability (#32) — sticking caused machine jams. The misting solution resolved that by changing the film-pizza interface (Principles #35, #10, #5). 2) The recyclability constraint introduces a new worsening parameter: Compatibility/Connectivity (#33). The misting bar integrated with the film guide and the film with food residue create a connected, inseparable assembly that hinders material recovery. 3) The TRIZ matrix for (39,33) yields principles #35, #10, #40, #1. 4) Principle #35 Parameter changes: instead of adding a physical steam layer, change the film material itself to have a permanent non-stick surface that doesn't require a separate misting system. 5) Principle #10 Preliminary action: pre-treat the pizza surface with a dry, edible, recyclable-compatible powder (e.g., starch) that acts as a release agent and washes off during recycling without contaminating the film. 6) Principle #40 Composite materials: use a mono-material film with a thin, recyclable inner coating that is chemically compatible with the base polymer, so the entire film can be recycled as one material. 7) Principle #1 Segmentation: design the film wrapping process so that any food-contact layer is a segmented, easily detachable liner that can be separated after use, leaving a clean recyclable outer film. 8) The new solution must avoid adding non-recyclable components and ensure the film remains free of contaminants that would prevent recycling.
cases.solution: Replace the standard shrink film with a mono-material PE film that has a thin, co-extruded starch-based inner coating. The coating acts as a permanent non-stick surface when in contact with hot cheese, eliminating the need for a misting bar. The entire film, including the coating, is polyethylene-based and can be recycled in the PE stream without separation. The pizza surface is dry-dusted with a fine, food-grade starch prior to wrapping to further ensure release; any starch residue on the film is water-soluble and removed during the recycling wash process, leaving pure PE for recovery.

cases.problem: BUG ID: PKG-2247-DEEP. SEVERITY: Critical. Line 4 pharmaceutical blister pack thermoformer. Current throughput: 180 cycles/min. Target: 250 cycles/min. Primary contradiction resolved by adding an IR pre-heat module: PVC web is uniformly pre-heated to 100 °C, allowing the contact oven to add the final 40 °C in 0.8 seconds, enabling 250 cycles/min with <1% thin-wall defects. However, a hidden constraint emerges: the IR pre-heat module consumes 18 kW of additional power, causing the overall machine power density to exceed the facility's 150 kW circuit capacity by 12% during peak demand. This triggers circuit breaker trips and voltage sags, compromising the stability of the entire packaging line. Simply upgrading the electrical infrastructure is cost-prohibitive (quoted at $220,000) and requires a 6-week shutdown. The contradiction is that increasing productivity (speed) now worsens energy efficiency and system reliability, creating a secondary bottleneck.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction (39 vs 32): Productivity vs Manufacturability — resolved by IR pre-heat module using principles #35, #10, #5. 2) Secondary contradiction emerges: improving Productivity (#39) now worsens Energy spent by moving object (#19) because the IR module increases total power draw beyond facility limits, causing reliability issues. 3) TRIZ matrix for (39,19) recommends principles #2, #35, #10. 4) Principle #2 Taking out: separate the energy-intensive pre-heating function from the main machine and power it via a dedicated, lower-power thermal storage system. 5) Principle #35 Parameter changes: alter the energy form — use a phase-change material (PCM) thermal battery that charges slowly during off-peak hours and discharges rapidly during production, delivering the needed 100 °C pre-heat without drawing peak power from the grid. 6) Principle #10 Preliminary action: charge the PCM thermal battery with waste heat recovered from the contact oven’s exhaust and from the facility’s air compressors, pre-accumulating energy before the production shift starts. 7) Combined, the PCM thermal battery replaces the direct IR electrical load, reducing peak power draw by 16 kW (from 18 kW to 2 kW for controls), keeping the total below 150 kW. The pre-heating function is maintained, enabling 250 cycles/min without electrical infrastructure upgrades.
cases.solution: Replace the direct IR pre-heat module with a phase-change material (PCM) thermal battery integrated into the web path. The PCM battery stores waste heat from the contact oven exhaust and facility air compressors during off-peak hours. During production, it releases stored heat to uniformly pre-heat the PVC web to 100 °C via a conductive roller system, consuming only 2 kW for temperature regulation. This eliminates the 18 kW IR peak load, keeps total power draw under 150 kW, and maintains 250 cycles/min throughput with <1% defects.

cases.problem: I'm the plant manager at a detergent pod factory, and I'm pulling my hair out over this. Our new multi-lane sachet machine is supposed to do 1,200 pods a minute, but the guys on the floor have to crawl inside it every two hours to scrape dried gel off the heat-sealing jaws. It's sticky, messy, and the downtime is making us miss shipments. I told the engineer to just speed up the line to make up for it, but he says that makes the jaws gunk up even faster, and then we get leaky pods that burst in the box. There's gotta be a way to run fast AND keep the machine clean! To make matters worse, our new corporate sustainability mandate requires all production lines to operate with zero VOC emissions in a clean-room environment, which rules out any solvent-based coatings or sprays we might have used to treat the jaws. The PTFE coating we considered releases trace VOCs during curing and wear, so that's off the table. We need a solution that eliminates jaw fouling without introducing any volatile organic compounds into the air.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original problem is unchanged: improve Productivity (#39) without worsening Manufacturability (#32) due to gel buildup on sealing jaws. 2) The constraint (zero VOC emissions, clean-room) invalidates the parent's solution of a PTFE coating, because application and wear release VOCs. This forces a re-evaluation of the contradiction. 3) The TRIZ matrix for (39,32) still suggests principles #35, #10, #5. However, #5 (Merging) in the form of a permanent non-stick coating is blocked by the VOC constraint, so we must look deeper. 4) We re-interpret the worsening parameter: Manufacturability (#32) is worsened not just by cleaning downtime but by the incompatibility of the coating process with clean-room standards. 5) We therefore seek principles that eliminate the need for a coating entirely. Principle #2 (Taking out) suggests removing the problematic part: instead of coating the jaws, take out the jaws from the sealing process. Principle #14 (Spheroidality) suggests using a curved surface to minimize contact area. Principle #35 (Parameter changes) can be applied to the gel itself: change its rheology to be less sticky at sealing temperature. Principle #10 (Preliminary action) can be used to pre-treat the gel, not the jaws. 6) The combination of #2 and #14 leads to a contactless sealing method, which inherently avoids fouling and requires no coatings, thus satisfying the VOC constraint.
cases.solution: Replace the heated mechanical jaws with a non-contact ultrasonic sealing system using curved, polished titanium sonotrodes. The gel is pre-cooled via a Peltier chiller just before sealing to increase its viscosity and reduce stickiness. The sonotrodes oscillate at high frequency to generate localized heat within the film layers, melting the sealant without ever touching the detergent gel. No coatings are needed, and the process emits zero VOCs, enabling continuous 1,200 ppm operation in a clean-room environment.

cases.problem: BUG ID: PKG-2247. SEVERITY: Critical. Line 4 pharmaceutical blister pack thermoformer. Current throughput: 180 cycles/min. Target: 250 cycles/min. When forming speed exceeds 210 cycles/min, the PVC web does not uniformly heat to glass transition temperature (target 140±3 °C) in the 1.2-second contact oven. Temperature gradient across web width reaches 12 °C, causing 22% of blister cavities to exhibit thin walls (<0.25 mm) and fail burst strength test (>15 N). Slowing the index to 180 cycles/min restores quality but misses production quota by 28%. The proposed solution of an integrated IR pre-heat module adds $2.80 per unit material cost due to specialized IR emitters, gold-plated reflectors, and additional control electronics, exceeding the mandatory cost ceiling of <$2 per unit material cost at an annual production volume of ≥1,000,000 units. This cost overrun makes the solution economically nonviable for high-volume manufacturing.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original root cause (insufficient heat transfer time at high speed) remains, but the constraint introduces a strict material cost ceiling (<$2/unit) that the original IR pre-heat solution violates. 2) The improving parameter is still Productivity (#39) – achieving 250 cycles/min. 3) The worsening parameter is still Manufacturability (#32) – the process becomes unreliable and now cost-prohibitive due to expensive pre-heat components; the solution cannot be economically manufactured at scale. 4) The original TRIZ contradiction matrix for (39,32) recommends principles #35, #10, #5. However, the cost constraint eliminates #10 (Preliminary action) because adding an expensive external pre-heat module directly conflicts with the <$2/unit target. 5) A fresh matrix lookup for (39,32) also yields principles #28 (Mechanics substitution) and #24 (Intermediary) as secondary recommendations, which become more applicable under the cost constraint. 6) Apply #35 Parameter change: instead of adding costly IR emitters, change the physical parameter of the PVC web itself – use a thinner, lower-cost PVC formulation with a lower glass transition temperature (e.g., 120°C) that heats faster and more uniformly in the existing contact oven. 7) Apply #28 Mechanics substitution: replace the purely conductive contact oven with a hybrid system that uses forced hot air convection (a mechanical fluid) to enhance heat transfer uniformity without expensive IR components. 8) Apply #24 Intermediary: introduce a thin, disposable, low-cost heat-transfer film (e.g., aluminum-coated paper) between the contact oven plates and the PVC web to improve thermal contact and uniformity, which can be produced at <$0.15 per unit. 9) Apply #5 Merging: integrate the forced air channels directly into the existing contact oven plates, eliminating the need for a separate pre-heat module and reducing overall material cost. 10) This combination achieves uniform heating to the required temperature at 250 cycles/min while keeping material costs below $2 per unit by avoiding expensive IR components and leveraging low-cost material modifications.
cases.solution: Switch to a thinner, lower-glass-transition PVC formulation (120°C Tg) and retrofit the contact oven with integrated forced hot air channels (using existing plant compressed air heated via a low-cost heat exchanger). A disposable aluminum-coated paper interleaf is fed between the oven plates and PVC web to ensure uniform thermal contact. This achieves uniform 140°C heating in 0.9 seconds, enabling 250 cycles/min with <1% defects, at a total material cost of $1.85 per unit.

cases.problem: The depositor manifold with integrated cooling jacket successfully eliminates splashing by increasing product viscosity, but this solution introduces a hidden constraint: the cooled, high-viscosity product now exhibits significantly slower flow dynamics and requires higher pressure to achieve the same fill speed, leading to shear-induced product degradation (e.g., breaking of sauce particulates or emulsion separation) and increased energy consumption. This creates a secondary trade-off between maintaining product quality (minimizing degradation) and the achievable throughput, as the elevated pressure and shear rates needed to push the thickened product through the nozzles at high speed risk damaging the ready-meal's texture and appearance.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: Improving Productivity (#39) worsens Manufacturability (#32) due to splashing. Matrix lookup for (39,32) yields #35, #10, #5. Solution applied: integrated cooling jacket to increase viscosity and eliminate splash. 2) Secondary contradiction emerges: the increased viscosity from cooling, while solving splashing, now worsens Object-generated harmful factors (#31) — the high pressure needed to push the cooled product causes shear degradation, harming product quality. Matrix lookup for (39,31) yields #35 (Parameter changes), #28 (Mechanics substitution), #10 (Preliminary action). 3) Principle #35 is extended: instead of cooling the entire product, we temporarily change the rheological parameter only at the nozzle exit by applying a controlled vibration (e.g., ultrasonic) to reduce viscosity just at the point of deposition, allowing low-pressure flow while retaining anti-splash plug formation. 4) Principle #28 replaces purely mechanical pressure with a non-mechanical field: use a peristaltic or progressive cavity pump with a pulsation dampener to deliver the product in a gentle, low-shear manner, avoiding degradation. 5) Principle #10 is applied by pre-treating the product with a small amount of a reversible, food-grade thickener (e.g., xanthan gum) that forms a shear-thinning gel; the gel is thick at rest (preventing splash) but thins under the low shear of pumping, then instantly recovers viscosity upon deposition. 6) Combined, these principles resolve the secondary contradiction: high throughput is maintained without product degradation, and the original splash issue remains solved.
cases.solution: A depositor system that uses a shear-thinning, food-grade gel pre-mixed into the ready-meal. The product is pumped gently using a low-shear progressive cavity pump. At the nozzle exit, a brief ultrasonic pulse temporarily reduces viscosity for clean release, after which the gel instantly recovers its structure, forming a cohesive plug that eliminates splash. This enables maximum sealing speeds without compromising product integrity.

cases.problem: Achieving high throughput in automated tray-sealing of viscous ready-meals is constrained by the filling accuracy of multi-head depositors. At elevated line speeds, product splashing onto the tray flange compromises the subsequent heat-seal integrity, necessitating a reduction in cycle rate to ensure seal quality. The original solution of integrating a cooling jacket into the depositor manifold to increase product viscosity relies on a refrigeration system using a synthetic refrigerant (e.g., R-134a or R-404A), which is now prohibited under EU F-Gas Regulation (EU) No 517/2014 due to high global warming potential. Alternative natural refrigerants like ammonia or CO₂ are either toxic or require high-pressure equipment, introducing safety and cost barriers. This creates a new conflict: maintaining the physical parameter change (viscosity increase) without using hazardous or environmentally regulated substances, while still eliminating flange splash to sustain high seal cycle rates.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction remains: improving Productivity (#39) worsens Manufacturability (#32) due to splash-induced seal defects. The TRIZ matrix lookup for (39,32) yields principles #35, #10, #5 as before. 2) However, the constraint (F-Gas Regulation) prohibits the hazardous HFC refrigerants used in the original #35 solution (cooling to change viscosity). This forces a re-evaluation: we must achieve the same parameter change (viscosity increase) without environmentally harmful substances. 3) Principle #35 (Parameter changes) is reinterpreted: instead of changing temperature, we change the physical state by altering pressure or applying a mechanical field. 4) Principle #28 (Mechanics substitution) is introduced because we replace a thermodynamic method (refrigeration) with a mechanical system (e.g., vibration, shear, or electrostatic field) to control fluid behavior, avoiding regulated chemicals entirely. 5) Principle #13 (The other way round) is added to invert the action: rather than cooling the product to prevent splash, we could heat the tray flange slightly to instantly evaporate any micro-splash, or use a reverse-timing approach where the depositor moves closer to the tray during filling. 6) The updated principle set (#35, #10, #5, #28, #13) addresses the core contradiction while complying with environmental regulations. #28 is the key addition, enabling a non-chemical, non-thermal solution.
cases.solution: A depositor manifold with an integrated piezoelectric vibration unit that applies high-frequency, low-amplitude oscillation to the product at the nozzle tip. The vibration increases the extensional viscosity of the viscous ready-meal during deposition, forming a clean, cohesive plug without temperature change. This eliminates flange splash, requires no refrigerants, and uses only RoHS-compliant electronic components, enabling maximum heat-seal cycle rates.

cases.problem: Refrigerant compressor R-515B test unit fails to meet COP target. At condensing pressure target of 45 bar, measured suction volume flow drops to 0.0023 m³/s, 62% below the required 0.0061 m³/s. Single-screw compressor, 2900 RPM, displacement 0.008 m³/rev. Vibration amplitude exceeds 12 mm/s at discharge port. The system must be redesigned for subsea deployment, requiring all pressure-containing components to withstand 200 bar external hydrostatic pressure per ISO 13628-6 (subsea production control systems). The original tandem twin-screw solution cannot be directly submerged because the intermediate port and inter-stage cooling lines would collapse under 200 bar external pressure, and the segmented housing creates multiple sealing interfaces vulnerable to water ingress.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction remains: improving discharge pressure (#11) drastically reduces suction volume flow (#7). The matrix lookup for Pressure vs. Volume of moving object yields principles #1, #3, #19, which led to the tandem screw solution. 2) The subsea constraint (200 bar external pressure) introduces a new failure mode: the segmented housing and intermediate port of the tandem design cannot withstand external collapse without massive wall thickness, which would make the compressor too large and heavy for subsea deployment. External pressure also threatens sealing integrity. 3) This forces a re-evaluation of the contradiction: we must still improve pressure (#11) without sacrificing volume (#7), but now the solution must also survive high external pressure. 4) Principle #35 Parameter change is added because we can change the physical state of the pressure boundary—instead of a thin-walled segmented housing, use a thick-walled monolithic pressure vessel that resists external collapse. 5) Principle #40 Composite materials is added to address weight: a carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer overwrap on a thin metallic liner provides high collapse resistance with lower weight than a solid metal housing. 6) The original principles are retained but adapted: #1 Segmentation is now applied internally—the screw itself is segmented into stages within a single pressure-containing envelope, avoiding external segmentation. #3 Local quality is used to optimize lobe profiles inside the monocoque housing. #19 Periodic action is implemented via an internal pulsed injection system that does not require external ports vulnerable to collapse.
cases.solution: A subsea-rated monocoque compressor housing made of a duplex stainless steel liner with a carbon-fiber overwrap, containing a single rotor with two screw stages machined on a common shaft. The low-pressure stage uses a 5/6 lobe profile, the high-pressure stage a 4/5 lobe profile, with an internal inter-stage desuperheating ring fed by a pulsed liquid refrigerant injection system entirely contained within the pressure boundary. All external connections are minimized and use API 17D subsea connectors.

cases.problem: In cascaded sorption compressors for thermal energy storage, achieving high discharge pressure via segmented beds with graded porosity introduces a secondary conflict: the staggered thermal wave regeneration required for continuous flow (improving parameter) drastically increases the complexity of the thermal management system, leading to non-uniform temperature distribution and thermal stresses that compromise the structural stability of the sorbent beds (worsening parameter).
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The primary contradiction is Pressure (#11) vs. Volume of moving object (#7), resolved by segmentation and local quality, but this introduces a need for staggered thermal cycling to maintain continuous flow. 2) The secondary contradiction emerges: to achieve high productivity (continuous high-pressure flow, improving parameter #39), the system requires complex, rapid thermal cycling which induces severe thermal gradients, increasing internal stress and risk of bed fracture (worsening parameter #11, here reinterpreted as mechanical stress on the sorbent structure). 3) TRIZ matrix lookup for improving Productivity (#39) and worsening Stress or pressure (#11) yields principles #35 Parameter changes, #24 Intermediary, and #19 Periodic action. 4) Principle #35 Parameter changes suggests altering the physical state of the thermal medium: using a liquid-metal heat pipe instead of direct gas heating to uniformize temperature distribution and reduce gradients. 5) Principle #24 Intermediary introduces a compliant, thermally conductive graphite foam scaffold within each sorbent bed to absorb differential expansion and conduct heat evenly. 6) Principle #19 Periodic action is refined to a resonant thermal pulse pattern that matches the natural thermal relaxation time of the beds, minimizing stress amplitude while maintaining the staggered sequence from the parent solution. 7) Together, these principles maintain the high-pressure, high-flow output while preserving bed integrity over prolonged cyclic operation.
cases.solution: A cascaded sorption compressor with three beds embedded in graphite foam scaffolds, thermally coupled via sodium heat pipes to a resonant pulsed heating system, delivering continuous 15 bar ammonia at 0.05 kg/s with bed stress below 15 MPa fatigue limit.

cases.problem: I bought this air compressor for my workshop to run a sandblaster and a plasma cutter, and it's just not cutting it. The tank pressure drops to nothing the second I start blasting, and then it takes forever to build back up. The manual says it's a 10-bar compressor but the flow is pathetic — only 200 liters a minute. I need 10 bar AND at least 500 liters a minute. Can't you make one that actually does both? But here's the catch: my workshop operates under EU jurisdiction, so the compressor must fully comply with RoHS (2011/65/EU) and REACH (EC 1907/2006) regulations — no lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, or restricted phthalates in any component, including seals, lubricants, and coatings. The original design used lead-based bearing alloys and cadmium-plated fasteners for high-pressure durability, but those are now banned. How can we achieve the same performance without these hazardous materials?
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction remains: improving Pressure (#11) worsens Volume of moving object (#7) in a single-piston design. The matrix still suggests #1, #3, #19. 2) However, the RoHS/REACH constraint introduces a secondary contradiction: the original solution's materials (lead-based bearings, cadmium plating) are banned, and substituting them typically worsens durability (Object-generated harmful factors #30) or reliability (#27). This triggers additional principles from the matrix for improving harmful factors vs. volume or pressure. 3) Principle #28 Mechanics substitution becomes critical: we must replace the mechanical wear-prone lead/cadmium interfaces with non-hazardous alternatives, such as advanced polymer coatings or magnetic levitation. 4) Principle #35 Parameter changes is also needed: we can alter the physical state of the lubricant (e.g., using a solid, self-lubricating composite) or change the operating temperature to reduce wear without restricted additives. 5) The original #1, #3, #19 are retained for the flow/pressure contradiction but must be implemented with compliant materials. 6) Thus, the evolved solution uses a multi-piston segmented design (#1) with locally optimized bore sizes (#3) and a pulsed unloader (#19), but all high-wear surfaces are treated with a diamond-like carbon (DLC) coating (#28) and the crankcase uses a REACH-compliant synthetic ester lubricant with ceramic nanoparticle additives (#35) to achieve the required durability without hazardous substances.
cases.solution: A three-piston reciprocating compressor with RoHS-compliant construction: two large-bore aluminum pistons with DLC-coated rings for first-stage high-volume intake, one small-bore stainless steel piston with DLC coating for second-stage 10-bar compression, a common inter-stage manifold with a rotary unloader valve, and a crankcase lubricated with a biodegradable synthetic ester containing boron nitride nanoparticles for extreme-pressure wear protection, eliminating all restricted heavy metals and phthalates.

cases.problem: We scaled up the multi-point pulsed injection system and it worked great—tripled our bio-plastic precursor output with no foam. But then we hit a wall: the 12 injection nozzles kept getting clogged with bacterial biofilms because the intermittent flow allowed stagnant periods where cells adhered and grew. Cleaning meant shutting down the whole tank, which tanked our productivity gains. We needed to keep the high feed rate and foam control, but now the pulsed, distributed system itself was causing maintenance shutdowns.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: To improve Productivity (#39) by increasing sugar feed rate, we caused Object-generated harmful factors (#31) – foam and byproducts. The parent solution used principles #1 Segmentation, #19 Periodic action, and #35 Parameter changes (multi-point pulsed injection with pH buffer) to overcome this. 2) However, implementing that solution introduced a secondary contradiction: the pulsed, distributed injection system improved Reliability (#27) of the fermentation process by preventing foam, but the intermittent flow caused biofilm clogging in nozzles, leading to frequent cleaning shutdowns, which increased Loss of Time (#25). 3) For the secondary contradiction (#27 vs. #25), the TRIZ matrix suggests principles: #25 Self-service, #2 Taking out, #35 Parameter changes. 4) Combining these: #25 Self-service – design the nozzles to be self-cleaning during operation, using the flow itself to prevent biofilm. #2 Taking out – separate the problematic biofilm accumulation from the critical injection function by using a sacrificial or removable element. #35 Parameter changes – alter the physical state or properties of the interface (e.g., surface energy, temperature, or flow regime) to inhibit adhesion. 5) Together, these principles resolve both contradictions: we maintain high productivity and foam control (primary) while eliminating nozzle clogging and downtime (secondary) by making the injection system self-maintaining.
cases.solution: We redesigned the injection nozzles with a self-cleaning, two-stage geometry. Each nozzle has a spring-loaded inner sleeve that retracts during the 'off' pulse, exposing a slippery PTFE surface that sheds any biofilm. During the 'on' pulse, the sleeve extends and the sugar flow scrubs the tip. Additionally, we periodically reverse-flush a tiny amount of sterile buffer through each nozzle using the same pulse timer, physically ejecting any adhered cells. No manual cleaning needed, and we maintain triple feed rate.

cases.problem: A high-throughput pharmaceutical hydrogenation autoclave processes 500 kg of API intermediate per batch. The primary solution uses microencapsulated catalyst, pulsed hydrogen injection, and supercritical CO2 to prevent hot-spots and tar formation. However, the pulsed pressure-swing injection cycles (Periodic action) and supercritical CO2 co-solvent cause rapid pressure fluctuations (±15 bar) and phase-instability in the 2 m³ vessel, leading to cavitation-induced erosion of the microcapsule shells and premature catalyst release. This compromises the uniform temperature profile and reintroduces localized exotherms. The system must maintain mechanical integrity of the catalyst delivery system while preserving the ±2°C thermal uniformity and 99.5% yield.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: Improving Productivity (#39) via high catalyst loading worsens Object-generated harmful factors (#31) from tar formation. Parent solution uses #1 Segmentation (microencapsulated catalyst), #19 Periodic action (pulsed H2 injection), and #35 Parameter changes (supercritical CO2). 2) Hidden secondary contradiction emerges: To maintain productivity, the pulse frequency is increased to enhance mixing and thermal diffusivity, improving Speed (#9) of reaction and heat removal. However, rapid pressure swings (±15 bar) from pulsed injection and supercritical phase transitions cause cavitation, which worsens Stress or pressure (#11) on the microcapsules, leading to shell erosion and premature catalyst release. 3) TRIZ matrix lookup for secondary contradiction (#9, #11) yields Inventive Principles #14 Spheroidality, #28 Mechanics substitution, and #35 Parameter changes (already used). 4) Combine principles: #14 Spheroidality – redesign microcapsules as perfect spheres with a compliant, elastic shell to resist cavitation collapse forces. #28 Mechanics substitution – replace mechanical pressure-swing injection with an acoustic or electromagnetic field to modulate hydrogen solubility without bulk pressure changes. #35 Parameter changes – adjust supercritical CO2 state to a near-critical liquid-like density where phase boundaries are diffuse, eliminating sharp pressure waves. 5) Together, these resolve both contradictions: the elastic spherical capsules (#14) survive the gentler, field-induced hydrogen modulation (#28), and the tuned supercritical phase (#35) avoids cavitation. This preserves thermal uniformity and prevents tar while maintaining high productivity.
cases.solution: Replace the brittle polymer microcapsules with compliant, perfectly spherical elastomeric shells (e.g., PDMS) that deform reversibly under pressure fluctuations. Substitute the mechanical pressure-swing injection with a tuned ultrasonic field that oscillates hydrogen solubility via acoustic cavitation control, eliminating bulk pressure cycles. Operate the supercritical CO2 at a slightly subcritical density (0.6–0.8 g/mL) to blur phase boundaries and dampen pressure transients. The integrated system maintains ±2°C uniformity, 99.5% yield, and zero capsule failure over 1000 cycles.

cases.problem: A high-throughput pharmaceutical hydrogenation autoclave is required to process 500 kg of active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) intermediate per batch. Increasing the catalyst loading to improve reaction rate (Productivity, #39) results in excessive localized exotherms and the generation of harmful tar byproducts (Object-generated harmful factors, #31). The system must maintain a uniform temperature profile within ±2°C across the entire 2 m³ vessel. Under the EU Ecodesign Regulation 2024, the entire reactor system, including all consumables such as the microencapsulated catalyst and supercritical CO2 co-solvent, must be fully recyclable at end-of-life. The original solution's thermally-responsive polymer shells and supercritical CO2 phase present significant recycling challenges: the polymer degrades into non-recyclable microplastics and the supercritical CO2 infrastructure requires complex separation and recovery that is energy-prohibitive in a closed-loop recycling context.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction pair (Productivity vs. Object-generated harmful factors) remains valid, but the constraint introduces a secondary contradiction: the original solution's use of consumable polymer shells and supercritical CO2 conflicts with end-of-life recyclability, which maps to worsening parameter 'Ease of repair and recycling' (#34) or 'Amount of substance' (#26) if we consider material waste. 2) A TRIZ matrix lookup for the primary pair (#39, #31) still suggests #1 Segmentation, #19 Periodic action, #35 Parameter changes. However, to resolve the recycling constraint, we must also consider the pair (Productivity #39 vs. Ease of repair and recycling #34), which yields principles #1 Segmentation, #28 Mechanics substitution, #34 Discarding and recovering, #10 Preliminary action. 3) Principle #34 Discarding and recovering is directly relevant: it suggests designing the catalyst and solvent systems to be easily separated and recovered for reuse, rather than being discarded as waste. 4) Principle #5 Merging (from pair #39 vs. #26, Amount of substance) can be applied to integrate the catalyst with a recyclable support that also enhances heat transfer, merging functions to eliminate separate consumables. 5) The original principles #1 and #19 are retained but re-interpreted: Segmentation now refers to a reusable, segmented catalyst support (e.g., metallic foam cartridges) that can be easily removed and recycled; Periodic action uses hydrogen pulsing without requiring polymer encapsulation. 6) Principle #35 Parameter changes is shifted from supercritical CO2 to a recyclable ionic liquid or a switchable hydrophilicity solvent that can be recovered and reused in a closed loop. 7) The new combination ensures high productivity without tars while meeting the recyclability mandate.
cases.solution: Replace the microencapsulated catalyst with a reusable, segmented catalyst system: the catalyst is deposited on 3D-printed, high-thermal-conductivity metallic foam cartridges (e.g., aluminum or copper alloy) that are arranged in a modular array within the reactor. These cartridges are easily removed and fully recyclable at end-of-life. Hydrogen is introduced via a pulsed, pressure-swing injection profile managed by a thermal feedback loop to prevent hot-spots. Instead of supercritical CO2, use a recyclable switchable hydrophilicity solvent (SHS) that can be separated from the product and catalyst by CO2 bubbling at mild conditions, then recovered and reused in a closed loop. The SHS enhances heat transfer and viscosity reduction. After the reaction, the catalyst cartridges are regenerated and recycled, and the SHS is recycled with >99% efficiency. This achieves 99% yield with no tar formation and full compliance with the EU Ecodesign Regulation.

cases.problem: We scaled up the bio-plastic precursor fermentation by using a multi-point pulsed nutrient injection skid with pH buffering to prevent foam and boost productivity. However, new product stewardship requirements mandate compliance with RoHS and REACH regulations, meaning no hazardous substances can be used in the process or final product. The current pH buffer contains borate compounds, which are classified as Substances of Very High Concern (SVHC) under REACH due to reproductive toxicity. We cannot simply substitute the buffer without risking loss of pH control, which would bring back the original foam and harmful byproduct issues, negating the productivity gains.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction was Productivity (#39) vs. Object-generated harmful factors (#31), solved with Segmentation, Periodic action, and Parameter changes. The new constraint (REACH compliance) introduces a secondary contradiction: removing the hazardous borate buffer (to reduce harmful factors, #31) worsens pH control, which would decrease Productivity (#39) by allowing foam and byproducts to return. Effectively, the constraint forces us to resolve the same parameter pair but with a tighter solution space. 2) A fresh TRIZ matrix lookup for #39 vs. #31 still yields #1 Segmentation, #19 Periodic action, and #35 Parameter changes. However, #1 and #19 are already fully exploited in the physical hardware design, and the constraint specifically targets the chemical composition, making #35 Parameter changes the most relevant to adapt. To meet the constraint, we must find non-hazardous alternatives, which requires changing the parameter (buffer chemistry) while avoiding the harmful foam. 3) The constraint invalidates the original buffer (borate) and pushes us to explore principles beyond the standard matrix. #28 Mechanics substitution suggests replacing a chemical means (hazardous buffer) with a physical or non-chemical method for pH control, such as using electric fields or membrane systems to manage proton gradients without adding substances. #22 Blessing in disguise transforms the previously harmful foam into a useful indicator or control element, e.g., using foam formation as a real-time signal to dynamically adjust feed rate or aeration, thus eliminating the need for a buffer entirely. 4) These principles are selected because they directly address the chemical hazard constraint while preserving or enhancing productivity, and they go beyond the original set by introducing non-chemical and feedback-based strategies.
cases.solution: We replaced the borate buffer injection with a two-part system: first, we installed an electrodialysis unit that continuously removes excess protons from localized zones near the injection points, maintaining pH without any added chemicals—an application of Mechanics substitution. Second, we integrated a foam sensor that detects early foam formation and triggers a micro-adjustment in the pulsed feed timing (reducing the 'on' period slightly for a few cycles), using the foam as a feedback signal per Blessing in disguise. This completely eliminates hazardous buffers, meets REACH, and sustains the threefold production increase.

cases.problem: Okay, so we fixed the scorching problem with those pelletized hardener additions—the kettle doesn't overheat anymore, and we're pushing batches faster. But now the maintenance crew is screaming at me because the automated hopper and pelletizer system is a nightmare to keep clean. The pellets stick together in the feeder when the ambient humidity fluctuates, and we're spending three hours between shifts scrubbing solidified gunk out of the nozzles. We traded a thermal problem for a mechanical fouling problem. I need a way to add the hardener in controlled stages without creating a sticky mess that kills our uptime.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Primary contradiction: increasing hardener addition rate to improve Productivity (#39) causes thermal degradation (Object-generated harmful factors, #31). Resolved via #1 Segmentation (pelletized addition), #19 Periodic action (timed pulses), #35 Parameter changes (modified amine blend). 2) Secondary contradiction emerges: the segmented, periodic addition system (intended to maintain Productivity, #39) introduces a fouling problem due to pellet stickiness, degrading Ease of operation (#33) via frequent cleaning shutdowns. Matrix lookup for #39 vs #33 recommends #1 Segmentation, #28 Mechanics substitution, #35 Parameter changes. 3) To resolve both contradictions simultaneously, we combine #1 Segmentation (further dividing the hardener into microencapsulated droplets), #35 Parameter changes (altering the hardener's physical state from solid pellets to a non-stick, shear-thinning fluid suspension), and #19 Periodic action (retained from primary solution but applied to a pulsed injection of the suspension). This eliminates solid handling entirely, preventing fouling while preserving staged addition. The microencapsulation (#1) isolates reactive components until release, and the fluid form (#35) ensures clean flow. Periodic injection (#19) maintains exotherm control.
cases.solution: We replaced the pelletized solid hardener with a stable, non-stick liquid suspension containing microencapsulated amine droplets in an inert carrier fluid. The suspension is injected in timed pulses via a self-cleaning nozzle. The microcapsules rupture upon mixing, releasing the hardener in stages, while the carrier fluid acts as a heat sink and lubricant, eliminating both scorching and fouling.

cases.problem: This study addresses the inherent process intensification limit in the exothermic synthesis of nitroglycerin, where traditional continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR) face a productivity ceiling imposed by the formation of shock-sensitive decomposition byproducts. Elevating reactant throughput to enhance manufacturing output invariably leads to amplified thermal gradients and the genesis of hazardous molecular species, presenting a critical safety and quality dichotomy. Additionally, the final reactor assembly must survive 10,000 g shock per MIL-STD-810, a requirement that directly conflicts with the fragile micro-segmented flow architecture (200-channel SiC chip) and pulsed-flow system, as the chip and associated microfluidic interconnects are susceptible to fracture under high mechanical shock, potentially causing catastrophic release of energetic materials.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction between Productivity (#39) and Object-generated harmful factors (#31) remains valid, as increasing throughput still risks generating shock-sensitive byproducts. 2) However, the MIL-STD-810 shock constraint introduces a secondary physical contradiction: the micro-segmented SiC chip (Principle #1) needed for thermal control is inherently brittle and fails under 10,000 g shock. This forces a reevaluation of the principle set. 3) A fresh TRIZ matrix lookup for the pair (#39, #31) still suggests Principles #1, #19, #35. Yet, #19 (Periodic action) is de-emphasized because pulsed flow adds dynamic pressure fluctuations that exacerbate stress on microchannels under shock. 4) To address the shock survivability while retaining micro-segmentation benefits, Principle #24 (Intermediary) is introduced: a compliant, energy-absorbing intermediary layer encapsulates the microreactor, decoupling the brittle chip from the shock load. 5) Principle #35 (Parameter changes) is retained but shifted from solvent system to structural damping properties of the encapsulation material. 6) Principle #40 (Composite materials) is added to design a shock-resistant composite housing that replaces the monolithic SiC chip with a fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composite (CMC) microchannel structure, maintaining thermal conductivity and chemical resistance while improving fracture toughness. 7) This integrated approach preserves the fourfold productivity increase and byproduct suppression while achieving MIL-STD-810 shock compliance.
cases.solution: A shock-hardened micro-segmented flow reactor is developed, featuring a silicon carbide fiber-reinforced silicon carbide (SiC/SiC) ceramic matrix composite chip with 200 microchannels, encapsulated in a viscoelastic polyurea damping layer and housed in a titanium alloy frame. The biphasic ionic liquid/dichloromethane system is retained for heat dissipation. The reactor achieves a 4x productivity increase over CSTR, eliminates shock-sensitive byproducts, and passes 10,000 g shock testing per MIL-STD-810 without structural failure or leakage.

cases.problem: Look, I’m just the shift supervisor, but I gotta tell you—this new recipe for the epoxy resin is a nightmare. The engineers want us to just dump in more hardener to speed up the curing and push more batches per shift. Okay, fine, I get it, more money. But the stuff starts smoking and turning into a brittle, burnt-smelling garbage halfway through the cycle! I can’t sell that. You’re giving me a choice: make more and have it be junk, or make less and have it be good. I need a way to run fast without the whole kettle catching fire, literally. And now the compliance team says the new hardener blend we used to fix the scorching has a restricted amine under REACH—they just flagged it as a Substance of Very High Concern (SVHC). So we can't use that special slow-kick amine anymore. We have to reformulate with only non-hazardous, REACH-compliant materials, but still get the same fast cure and no thermal degradation.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The original contradiction remains: increasing hardener addition rate to improve Productivity (#39) causes thermal degradation (Object-generated harmful factors, #31). The constraint does not change the core contradiction but narrows the solution space by eliminating the previously used hindered amine hardener. 2) A fresh TRIZ matrix lookup for #39 and #31 still yields #1 Segmentation, #19 Periodic action, #35 Parameter changes as primary principles.
